common_name: Creole Rose Garlic (Warm-Climate Softneck Garlic for Gulf South Gardens)

planting.note: Plant individual cloves (pointed end up, blunt end down) 1–2 inches deep and 6 inches apart in fall in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Creole garlic does NOT require the long cold vernalization needed by northern hardneck varieties — a key distinction making it one of the only garlic types that reliably forms full-size bulbs in zone 8-10.

directions: Plant individual cloves 1–2 inches deep, pointed end up, 4–6 inches apart in fall (October–November in zone 9) in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Creole Rose is a Creole-type garlic — one of the most important garlic types for Gulf South gardeners because it forms full, large bulbs without the cold-vernalization requirement that makes most northern hardneck garlic types unreliable in zone 8-10. While Rocambole, Purple Stripe, and Porcelain hardneck garlics require many weeks of cold temperatures below 40°F to form proper bulbs, Creole garlics originated in warm-climate regions of Spain and the American South and thrive with the mild winters of zone 8-10. 'Creole Rose' produces attractive bulbs with rose-pink to rosy-purple outer wrappers and large, flavorful, ivory cloves with a rich, complex, moderately sharp garlic flavor — excellent for roasting, where the flavor becomes sweet and nutty, and for raw preparations where its full-bodied character is distinctive. Plant in fall for spring harvest: in zone 9, plant October through December for a late March–May harvest when the lower leaves begin to brown and fall over. Allow at least two weeks of drying in a warm, airy location after harvest before storage. The scape (central flower stalk) of softneck types is not produced, unlike the decorative and edible scapes of hardneck types.

common_name: Covington Sweet Potato (NCSU-Developed Leading Eastern US Commercial Sweet Potato)

planting.note: Plant certified slips after the last frost when soil has warmed above 65°F in well-drained, sandy loam to loamy, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun; Covington produces the most uniform, symmetrical tubers of commonly available sweet potato varieties — the consistent shape reduces culling and improves yields relative to older varieties.

directions: Plant certified sweet potato slips after the last frost in well-drained, loamy to sandy loam, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Covington was developed by North Carolina State University and released in 2005 — and has rapidly become the dominant commercial sweet potato variety across the Eastern United States, particularly in North Carolina (the leading sweet potato production state) and throughout the Gulf South. The combination of outstanding tuber uniformity (straight, symmetrical, consistent-sized roots), excellent deep-orange flesh color, good sweetness, exceptional storage quality, and broad adaptability drove Covington's rapid adoption over the previously dominant Beauregard. The copper-orange skin over vivid orange flesh produces the familiar market sweet potato appearance, but the distinctive Covington quality is the remarkable uniformity — roots are notably more consistent in size and shape than Beauregard or older varieties, reducing culled roots and improving marketable yield. The eating quality is excellent: moist, sweet, flavorful orange flesh with good texture after baking, boiling, or roasting. Covington is resistant to Fusarium wilt and several other sweet potato pathogens important in southeastern production. Cure harvested tubers for 5–7 days at 85–90°F with high humidity to convert starches to sugars and develop maximum sweetness.

common_name: Moonbeam Coreopsis (Classic Pale Yellow Long-Blooming Perennial Thread-Leaf Coreopsis)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Moonbeam requires excellent drainage and lean soil — plants in heavy clay or overly fertile soil tend to be short-lived and flop open; raised beds and amended soil provide the drainage needed for long-term success in Gulf South clay soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Moonbeam is one of the most beloved and awarded garden perennials of the late 20th century — a Coreopsis verticillata cultivar that received the Perennial Plant of the Year award in 1992 and remains a gold standard for long-blooming, low-maintenance perennial gardens. The distinctive feature of 'Moonbeam' compared to other coreopsis is its unique pale primrose-yellow to cream-yellow flower color — softer and more subtle than the vivid golden-yellow of most coreopsis cultivars — combined with the fine-textured, thread-like foliage of C. verticillata that creates an airy, delicate visual mass very different from the coarser leaves of C. grandiflora and C. lanceolata. The extremely long bloom season — from early summer through frost with deadheading — and the soft yellow color that harmonizes with virtually every other garden color make Moonbeam one of the most versatile perennials for border combinations. It blooms most prolifically with lean soil and moderate drought stress — overly rich, moist conditions produce lush foliage and reduced bloom. In zone 9, Moonbeam performs best with afternoon shade protection during peak summer and requires well-drained soil to persist as a perennial rather than a short-lived annual-style planting.

common_name: Summer Wave Torenia (Heat-Tolerant Shade Wishbone Flower Annual)

planting.note: Plant container transplants after the last frost in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Summer Wave Torenia is one of the most heat-tolerant shade annuals for Gulf South gardens — maintaining good bloom through the humid summer months when impatiens and other shade annuals fail.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Summer Wave Torenia (Torenia fournieri) is a spreading, trailing shade annual producing small (1-inch) two-lipped, tubular flowers in blue-purple with white and yellow markings — the wishbone-shaped stamen arrangement that gives the family its common name visible within each flower. The 'Summer Wave' series was developed by Proven Winners specifically for the trailing, spreading habit that fills containers and ground-level shade plantings with cascading growth rather than the upright habit of older torenia varieties. In Gulf South gardens, torenia occupies an important ecological niche: a heat-tolerant shade annual for summer months when impatiens (the other primary summer shade annual) increasingly fails due to impatiens downy mildew disease that has devastated impatiens performance across the southeastern US. The shade tolerance and heat performance of Summer Wave Torenia make it one of the most reliable summer annuals for partial-shade positions in zone 9 where few flowering plants provide consistent bloom through July–September. The spreading, self-branching habit fills 18–24 inch containers without pinching, and plants in ground-level beds spread quickly to provide solid coverage. Colors in the Summer Wave series range from blue to lavender to large white to berry colors — all with the characteristic wishbone flower form.

common_name: Purple Globe Amaranth (Heat-Proof Deep Purple Clover-Like Annual for Hot Summers)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Purple Globe Amaranth is among the best summer annuals for sustained performance in brutal Gulf South summer heat and humidity — it actually performs better in August than June in zone 9.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Purple Globe Amaranth (Gomphrena globosa) produces compact, globe-shaped, clover-like flower heads in rich magenta-purple — papery, button-like structures (1/2–3/4 inch) that maintain their color indefinitely when dried, making globe amaranth one of the premier cut-and-dried flower annuals for summer gardens. The papery bracts retain their vivid purple color when dry because they are composed of pigmented, non-photosynthetic modified leaves rather than the true petals (which would bleach on drying) — the same quality that makes strawflowers, statice, and immortelle persist when dried. In the summer garden, globe amaranth is outstanding for one key quality: it genuinely thrives in the brutal Gulf South summer heat that collapses most other annuals in July and August. The vivid purple clover-like heads continue to produce without pause through extreme heat and humidity, providing consistent garden color when most other summer annuals have declined. Butterflies visit the flowers consistently through the bloom season. The dried flower heads retain their color for months and are outstanding in dry arrangements. Low fertility and good drainage extend bloom; excess nitrogen promotes leafy growth over flowers.
